perturbation.original_text: "Nuance Data" means any Data that is (i) owned by a Third Party and licensed to the Nuance Group as of immediately prior to the Distribution pursuant to a Nuance Data Agreement or (ii) owned by the Nuance Group as of immediately prior to the Distribution but subject to a Nuance Data Agreement, in each case (i) and (ii), which Data is used in the SpinCo Business as of immediately prior to the Distribution.
perturbation.changed_text: "Nuance Data" means data used by the Nuance Group.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the detailed definition of 'Nuance Data,' which includes data licensed from third parties under specific agreements, and replacing it with a broad definition, the original obligations related to third-party data use become unclear. This creates an in-text contradiction with Section 3.01(e), which references Schedule B for specific rights and obligations regarding Nuance Data use by the SpinCo Group and could include third-party obligations. After the perturbation, that clause references a definition that may no longer encompass the same type of data or associated obligations, thus creating uncertainty about the extent of the license granted and whether SpinCo remains subject to the restrictions and obligations detailed in Schedule B concerning third-party data.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (e) Nuance Data. The Parties acknowledge and agree that certain rights and obligations with respect to the use or benefit by the SpinCo Group of certain Nuance Data shall be as provided in Schedule B.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of "Nuance Data" by removing the reference to third-party data licensed to Nuance. This creates a direct contradiction with Section 3.01(e), which refers to Schedule B for rights and obligations regarding "Nuance Data," potentially including third-party data. The modified definition no longer clearly encompasses this third-party data, making it unclear whether the SpinCo Group's rights and obligations outlined in Schedule B still apply to the same scope of data. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: "SpinCo Data" means any Data that is (i) owned by a Third Party and licensed to the Nuance Group or SpinCo Group as of immediately prior to the Distribution pursuant to a SpinCo Data Agreement or (ii) owned by the Nuance Group or SpinCo Group as of immediately prior to the Distribution but subject to a SpinCo Data Agreement and (iii) Data owned by the Nuance Group or SpinCo Group and exclusively related to the SpinCo Business as of immediately prior to the Distribution.
perturbation.changed_text: "SpinCo Data" means data used by the SpinCo Group.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the detailed definition of 'SpinCo Data,' and replacing it with a broad definition, the original obligations related to third-party data use become unclear. This creates an in-text contradiction with Section 4.01(e), which references Schedule C for specific rights and obligations regarding SpinCo Data use by the Nuance Group and could include third-party obligations. After the perturbation, that clause references a definition that may no longer encompass the same type of data or associated obligations, thus creating uncertainty about the extent of the license granted and whether Nuance remains subject to the restrictions and obligations detailed in Schedule C concerning third-party data.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (e) SpinCo Data. The Parties acknowledge and agree that certain rights and obligations with respect to the use or benefit of the Nuance Group of certain SpinCo Data shall be as provided in Schedule C.
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by providing a broad and simplified definition of 'SpinCo Data', removes the specific criteria and limitations related to third-party data and agreements that were present in the original definition. This omission directly contradicts section (e), which references Schedule C for details regarding the use of 'certain SpinCo Data' by the Nuance Group, as those details in Schedule C would depend on the original, more detailed definition and understanding of which data is subject to those terms. Thus, the change creates uncertainty as to which data the obligations in Schedule C apply, making the clause unenforceable.
